annotations.label: Natural logarithm (base e)

anchor.snippet: its logarithm to the base of the mathematical constant e

annotations.note: Real.log is the natural logarithm, defined as the inverse of Real.exp on the positive reals.

annotations.label: Natural log as exponent of e

anchor.snippet: is the power to which e would have to be raised to equal x

annotations.note: Real.exp_log states exp (log x) = x for x > 0, i.e. log x is the exponent giving x.

annotations.label: ln 7.5

anchor.snippet: For example, ln 7.5 is 2.0149

annotations.note: Mathlib has no decimal evaluation of this specific value.

annotations.label: Natural log as area under 1/x

anchor.snippet: can be defined for any positive real number a as the area under the curve

annotations.note: integral_one_div_of_pos gives ∫ 1/x from a to b = log (b/a), so the area from 1 to a equals log a.

annotations.label: Inverse of exponential identities

anchor.snippet: is the inverse function of the exponential function

annotations.note: Real.exp_log (exp (log x) = x) and Real.log_exp (log (exp x) = x) express the mutual-inverse relation.

anchors.snippet: is the inverse function of the exponential function

annotations.label: Product rule (multiplication to addition)

anchor.snippet: the natural logarithm maps multiplication of positive numbers into addition

annotations.note: Real.log_mul states log (x*y) = log x + log y for nonzero x, y.

anchors.snippet: the natural logarithm maps multiplication of positive numbers into addition

annotations.label: Change of base

anchor.snippet: logarithms in other bases differ only by a constant multiplier from the natural logarithm

annotations.note: Real.log_div_log gives logb b x = log x / log b, i.e. base-b log is natural log times the constant 1/log b.

annotations.label: Inverse of exponential function

anchor.snippet: can be defined more generally as the inverse function of the exponential function

annotations.note: Real.log is literally defined via expOrderIso.symm and Real.log_exp confirms log (exp x) = x.

annotations.label: Area under rectangular hyperbola

anchor.snippet: can be defined as the area under the graph of a rectangular hyperbola

annotations.note: integral_inv gives ∫ x⁻¹ from a to b = log (b/a), the area under the hyperbola y = 1/x.

anchors.snippet: can be defined as the area under the graph of a rectangular hyperbola

annotations.label: Logarithm properties

anchor.snippet: obeys the following mathematical properties

annotations.note: The identity table (log_mul, log_div, log_pow, log_rpow, log_one) is fully present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: obeys the following mathematical properties

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\ln 1&=0,\\\ln e&=1,\\\ln(xy)&=\ln x+\ln y\quad {\text{for }}\;x>0\;{\text{and }}\;y>0,\\\ln(x/y)&=\ln x-\ln y\quad {\text{for }}\;x>0\;{\text{and }}\;y>0,\\\ln(x^{y})&=y\ln x\quad {\text{for }}\;x>0,\\\ln({\sqrt[{y}]{x}})&=(\ln x)/y\quad {\text{for }}\;x>0\;{\text{and }}\;y\neq 0,\\\ln x&<\ln y\quad {\text{for }}\;0<x<y.\end{aligned}}}

annotations.label: Limits at 0 and infinity

anchor.snippet: the natural logarithm approaches negative infinity

annotations.note: tendsto_log_nhdsGT_zero (log → atBot as x→0⁺) and tendsto_log_atTop (log → atTop) capture both limits.

anchors.snippet: the natural logarithm approaches negative infinity

annotations.label: Taylor expansion around 1

anchor.snippet: one looks for Taylor expansions around other points

annotations.note: Documented as the power series expansion of the logarithm around 1: ∑ xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) = -log(1-x) for |x|<1.

anchors.snippet: one looks for Taylor expansions around other points

annotations.label: Mercator series

anchor.snippet: A change of variables yields the Mercator series

annotations.note: The Mercator series log(1+x)=∑(-1)ⁿxⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) is the x↦-x substitution of this same Mathlib power-series lemma.

anchors.snippet: A change of variables yields the Mercator series

annotations.label: Harmonic series and Euler–Mascheroni

anchor.snippet: the harmonic series truncated at N is close to the logarithm of N

annotations.note: tendsto_harmonic_sub_log shows harmonic n - log n → eulerMascheroniConstant γ.

annotations.label: Special case series for positive integers

anchor.snippet: A useful special case for positive integers n

annotations.note: hasSum_log_one_add_inv gives log(1+a⁻¹) = 2∑ 1/(2k+1)·(1/(2a+1))^(2k+1), exactly the positive-integer special case.

anchors.snippet: A useful special case for positive integers n

anchor.snippet: can also be expressed as an infinite product

annotations.note: No infinite-product representation of Real.log is in Mathlib (only generic product/log summability machinery).

annotations.note: HasDerivAt.log gives d/dx log(g x) = g'/g (for g x ≠ 0), the differential form, but the integral statement ∫ g'/g = log|g|+C is not a named lemma.

anchor.snippet: where C is an arbitrary constant of integration

annotations.note: Real.hasDerivAt_log shows the derivative of log is x⁻¹ (with log = log|x|), so log|x| is the antiderivative of 1/x.

annotations.label: Example integral

anchor.snippet: over an interval that does not include points where

annotations.note: Such an example is a direct application of integral_inv, which requires 0 ∉ [[a,b]].

anchors.snippet: over an interval that does not include points where

anchors.value: {\displaystyle \int \tan x\,dx=\int {\frac {\sin x}{\cos x}}\,dx=-\int {\frac {{\frac {d}{dx}}\cos x}{\cos x}}\,dx=-\ln \left|\cos x\right|+C=\ln \left|\sec x\right|+C.}

annotations.label: Integration by parts of ln

anchor.snippet: can be integrated using integration by parts

annotations.note: integral_log gives ∫ log s = b log b - a log a - b + a, the integration-by-parts result.

anchors.snippet: can be integrated using integration by parts

annotations.label: Convergence near 1

anchor.snippet: the closer the value of x is to 1, the faster the rate of convergence

annotations.note: This informal convergence-rate remark is not formalized as a theorem.

anchor.snippet: a transcendental number approximately equal to 2.302 585 09

annotations.note: No decimal value or transcendence proof of log 10 is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complex exponential extension

anchor.snippet: can be extended to a function which gives a complex number

annotations.note: Complex.log is the complex logarithm (principal branch), the extension to the complex exponential.

annotations.label: Complex logarithm is multi-valued

anchor.snippet: the logarithm cannot be defined for the whole complex plane

annotations.note: Mathlib picks a single principal branch but exp_eq_exp_iff_exists_int / log_exp_exists capture the 2πi multivaluedness.

annotations.label: Values of ln i

anchor.snippet: 4 ln i can be defined as 2 iπ

annotations.note: Complex.log_I states log I = π/2 · I, so 4 log I = 2πi.
